title: TARDIVE DYSKINESIA Recognized at a Routine Community-Mental-Health Visit in a 63-Year-Old Woman with Long-Standing Schizophrenia on ~20 Years of a First-Generation Antipsychotic (Haloperidol) Plus Type 2 Diabetes — New, Persistent, INVOLUNTARY Orofacial-Buccolingual Movements (Lip Smacking, Puckering, Tongue Protrusion/Writhing, Facial Grimacing) and Choreoathetoid Finger Movements She Is Largely UNAWARE Of: DISTINGUISHING the Four Antipsychotic Movement Syndromes by TIMING (ACUTE DYSTONIA in Hours–Days, AKATHISIA in Days–Weeks, Drug-Induced PSEUDOPARKINSONISM in Weeks–Months, and TARDIVE DYSKINESIA After MONTHS–YEARS of Dopamine Blockade — Often PERSISTENT/Irreversible), QUANTIFYING with the AIMS (Abnormal Involuntary Movement Scale) and Ruling Out Mimics, NOTIFYING Psychiatry to Re-Evaluate/Minimize or SWITCH the Antipsychotic (NEVER an Abrupt Stop — Withdrawal-Emergent Dyskinesia + Relapse) and Start an FDA-Approved VMAT2 INHIBITOR (VALBENAZINE or DEUTETRABENAZINE) with QTc/Somnolence/Mood Monitoring, the Classic Test Point that ANTICHOLINERGICS (Benztropine/Diphenhydramine) WORSEN TD (They Treat Dystonia/Pseudoparkinsonism, NOT TD), and Swallowing/Safety + Non-Judgmental Psychosocial Support for a Stigmatizing, Quality-of-Life-Limiting Condition
subtitle: TARDIVE DYSKINESIA (TD) is a LATE ('tardive' = delayed) movement disorder caused by MONTHS-TO-YEARS of dopamine D2-receptor blockade (chronic antipsychotics — especially FIRST-GENERATION agents like haloperidol, but also second-generation agents and dopamine-blocking antiemetics such as metoclopramide/prochlorperazine), thought to arise from D2-receptor up-regulation/supersensitivity; it is frequently PERSISTENT and may be IRREVERSIBLE, so early recognition matters. This case follows B. Aldridge, a 63-year-old woman with long-standing schizophrenia maintained on haloperidol for about 20 years (with type 2 diabetes — older age, female sex, diabetes, mood disorder, and prior extrapyramidal symptoms all raise TD risk), seen for a routine long-acting injection when the nurse notices NEW, repetitive, INVOLUNTARY OROFACIAL-BUCCOLINGUAL-MASTICATORY movements (lip smacking and puckering, tongue protrusion/writhing, chewing, facial grimacing) plus slow CHOREOATHETOID finger/hand movements — present at rest, worse with distraction, absent in sleep, and movements the patient is largely UNAWARE of. The nurse must DISTINGUISH the four antipsychotic-associated movement syndromes by their TIMING and features: ACUTE DYSTONIA (hours–days; sustained spasms — torticollis, oculogyric crisis, laryngospasm = airway emergency) responds to an ANTICHOLINERGIC (benztropine) or diphenhydramine; AKATHISIA (days–weeks; subjective inner restlessness/inability to sit still) responds to dose reduction, a beta-blocker (propranolol), or a benzodiazepine; drug-induced PSEUDOPARKINSONISM (weeks–months; bradykinesia, cogwheel rigidity, resting tremor, masked facies) responds to dose reduction/switch or an anticholinergic; and TARDIVE DYSKINESIA (months–years; the picture here) is treated very differently. The nurse QUANTIFIES the movements with the AIMS (Abnormal Involuntary Movement Scale) — the standardized tool done at antipsychotic initiation and then periodically (more often with high-risk/first-generation agents) — and helps rule out mimics (Huntington disease, primary dystonias, stereotypies, ill-fitting dentures, metoclopramide exposure, withdrawal-emergent dyskinesia). MANAGEMENT: NOTIFY psychiatry to re-evaluate the ongoing need for and DOSE of the antipsychotic and consider SWITCHING to a lower-TD-risk agent — but NEVER abruptly STOP it (abrupt withdrawal can cause withdrawal-emergent dyskinesia and psychotic relapse; changes are tapered/adjusted by the prescriber) — and START an FDA-approved VMAT2 INHIBITOR (VALBENAZINE [Ingrezza] or DEUTETRABENAZINE [Austedo]), which reduce presynaptic dopamine release and are first-line for TD, monitoring for somnolence, QTc prolongation, parkinsonism, and mood/suicidality. A CLASSIC NCLEX TEST POINT: ANTICHOLINERGICS (benztropine, diphenhydramine, trihexyphenidyl) and simply INCREASING the antipsychotic do NOT help TD and can WORSEN or merely MASK it — anticholinergics are for acute dystonia/pseudoparkinsonism, not TD. The nurse also assesses SWALLOWING/aspiration and gait safety as movements can impair function, and provides NON-JUDGMENTAL psychosocial support for a stigmatizing, distressing, quality-of-life-limiting condition while reinforcing adherence and follow-up. Step-6 evaluates whether each later finding reflects the patient IMPROVING, WORSENING, or remaining UNCHANGED.
scenario: TARDIVE DYSKINESIA (TD) is a LATE ('tardive' = delayed) movement disorder caused by MONTHS-TO-YEARS of dopamine D2-receptor blockade (chronic antipsychotics — especially FIRST-GENERATION agents like haloperidol, but also second-generation agents and dopamine-blocking antiemetics such as metoclopramide/prochlorperazine), thought to arise from D2-receptor up-regulation/supersensitivity; it is frequently PERSISTENT and may be IRREVERSIBLE, so early recognition matters. The nurse also assesses SWALLOWING/aspiration and gait safety as movements can impair function, and provides NON-JUDGMENTAL psychosocial support for a stigmatizing, distressing, quality-of-life-limiting condition while reinforcing adherence and follow-up. Step-6 evaluates whether each later finding reflects the patient IMPROVING, WORSENING, or remaining UNCHANGED.

steps.q: Which of the following findings is MOST important for the nurse to recognize in this client?

opts.text: Subjective inner restlessness with constant pacing and inability to sit still
opts.rat: ⚠️ This describes AKATHISIA, a different syndrome — she does not report restlessness or pace.

opts.text: Repetitive involuntary lip smacking/puckering, tongue protrusion/writhing, and facial grimacing
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — orofacial-buccolingual-masticatory movements are the classic presentation of tardive dyskinesia.

opts.text: Sustained painful neck spasm with the eyes rolling upward (oculogyric crisis)
opts.rat: ⚠️ This describes ACUTE DYSTONIA (hours–days), not TD — she has none of these sustained spasms.

steps.q: Which statement BEST explains the significance of this client’s clinical findings?

opts.text: Giving benztropine now is the priority treatment for these movements
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Anticholinergics worsen TD; they are indicated for acute dystonia/pseudoparkinsonism, not tardive dyskinesia.

opts.text: Increasing the haloperidol dose is the best way to control the movements
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Raising the antipsychotic may transiently mask TD but worsens the underlying disorder.

opts.text: This is tardive dyskinesia — a late complication of months-to-years of dopamine blockade that may be persistent or irreversible
opts.rat: CORRECT — the late onset, orofacial choreoathetoid pattern, and chronic antipsychotic exposure define TD.

steps.q: Which is the MOST likely explanation for this client’s presentation?

opts.rat: #4 — Unlikely: dystonia is an early (hours–days) sustained spasm, not chronic choreoathetoid movements.
#3 — Also antipsychotic-related, but she has no subjective restlessness or inability to sit still.

opts.text: Tardive dyskinesia from long-term antipsychotic (dopamine-blockade) therapy
opts.rat: #1 — MOST likely: late-onset orofacial choreoathetoid movements after ~20 years of antipsychotics are classic TD.
#2 — On the antipsychotic-EPS differential, but she lacks bradykinesia, cogwheel rigidity, resting tremor, and masked facies.

steps.q:
Increase the haloperidol dose to suppress the movements

Based on this finding, the client’s status is BEST classified as:

opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — raising the antipsychotic only masks TD transiently and worsens the underlying disorder.

steps.q:
Abruptly stop the antipsychotic today to remove the cause

Based on this finding, the client’s status is BEST classified as:

opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — abrupt withdrawal can cause withdrawal-emergent dyskinesia and psychotic relapse; changes are tapered by the prescriber.

steps.q:
Notify psychiatry to re-evaluate and minimize the antipsychotic dose or switch to a lower-TD-risk agent

Based on this finding, the client’s status is BEST classified as:

opts.rat: Reducing dopamine-blockade exposure with a lower-risk agent is a core management step.

steps.q: Which action should the nurse take FIRST?

opts.text: Carry out the plan to minimize/switch the antipsychotic without abruptly stopping it and start the prescribed VMAT2 inhibitor
opts.rat: #3 — The definitive interventions, done safely to avoid relapse and withdrawal-emergent dyskinesia.

opts.text: Notify psychiatry/the prescriber with the findings and AIMS score
opts.rat: #2 — Escalation enables medication decisions (dose change/switch, starting a VMAT2 inhibitor).

opts.text: Assess swallowing/aspiration and gait safety and provide education and non-judgmental support
opts.rat: #4 — Protects function and supports adherence and coping with a visible condition.

opts.text: Recognize the movements as possible TD and complete an AIMS, distinguishing it from dystonia/akathisia/pseudoparkinsonism and ruling out mimics
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Accurate recognition and a baseline AIMS drive correct, safe management.

steps.q:
At six months the movements are markedly improved and she is engaged in care with regular AIMS monitoring

Based on this finding, the client’s status is BEST classified as:

opts.rat: Sustained reduction in movements with ongoing monitoring confirms effective, safe management.

steps.q:
After benztropine was added for the movements, the orofacial dyskinesia is now worse

Based on this finding, the client’s status is BEST classified as:

opts.rat: ⚠️ Anticholinergics worsen TD — stop the benztropine; it is not a treatment for tardive dyskinesia.

steps.q:
Two weeks into the VMAT2 inhibitor her AIMS score is about the same as baseline

Based on this finding, the client’s status is BEST classified as:

opts.rat: Benefit may take several weeks; continue therapy and reassess at the next interval.
